question_html: The magnetic field due to a magnetic dipole of magnetic moment {tex}M{/tex} at a point on the axis of the dipole and at a distance d from it is given by:
question_text: The magnetic field due to a magnetic dipole of magnetic moment {tex}M{/tex} at a point on the axis of the dipole and at a distance d from it is given by:

options.explanation: The solution is correct because the magnetic field {tex}B{/tex} due to a magnetic dipole at a point along its axis is derived from the dipole's magnetic moment {tex}M{/tex} . The formula for the magnetic field on the axis of a dipole at a distance {tex}d{/tex} is given by: {tex}B = \frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2M}{d^{3}}{/tex} This expression shows that the magnetic field strength decreases with the cube of the distance from the dipole, which is characteristic of dipole fields. The factor of 2 in the numerator arises from the specific geometry of the dipole field along its axis. Thus, option ( {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 M}{d^{3}}{/tex} ) accurately represents the magnetic field at the specified location.

The formula for the magnetic field on the axis of a dipole at a distance {tex}d{/tex} is given by: {tex}B = \frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2M}{d^{3}}{/tex} This expression shows that the magnetic field strength decreases with the cube of the distance from the dipole, which is characteristic of dipole fields. The factor of 2 in the numerator arises from the specific geometry of the dipole field along its axis. question_html: A rectangular loop of area {tex}\vec{A}{/tex}, carrying current {tex}I{/tex}, is placed in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex}. With the help of a suitable diagram, derive an expression, in vector form, for the torque acting on the loop.
question_text: A rectangular loop of area {tex}\vec{A}{/tex} , carrying current {tex}I{/tex} , is placed in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} . With the help of a suitable diagram, derive an expression, in vector form, for the torque acting on the loop.
explanation: Forces on the arms BC and DA are, equal opposite and collinear. Hence they will cancel each other. The forces on arms AB and CD are {tex}\vec{F}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{F}_2{/tex} , equal but not collinear. The magnitude of the torque on the loop is {tex} \tau =F_1 \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta+F_2 \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} =I a b B \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} =m B \sin \theta (m=I A) {/tex} {tex} \vec{\tau} =\vec{m} \times \vec{B} {/tex}
solution: Forces on the arms BC and DA are, equal opposite and collinear. Hence they will cancel each other. The forces on arms AB and CD are {tex}\vec{F}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{F}_2{/tex} , equal but not collinear. The magnitude of the torque on the loop is {tex} \tau =F_1 \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta+F_2 \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} =I a b B \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} =m B \sin \theta (m=I A) {/tex} {tex} \vec{\tau} =\vec{m} \times \vec{B} {/tex}
solution_html:
Forces on the arms BC and DA are, equal opposite and collinear. Hence they will cancel each other.
The forces on arms AB and CD are {tex}\vec{F}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{F}_2{/tex}, equal but not collinear. The magnitude of the torque on the loop is
{tex} \tau =F_1 \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta+F_2 \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta {/tex}
{tex} =I a b B \sin \theta {/tex}
{tex} =m B \sin \theta (m=I A) {/tex}
{tex} \vec{\tau} =\vec{m} \times \vec{B} {/tex}

question_html: Differentiate between magnetisation and the susceptibility of a material. What can you say about the susceptibility of paramagnetic and diamagnetic materials?
question_text: Differentiate between magnetisation and the susceptibility of a material. What can you say about the susceptibility of paramagnetic and diamagnetic materials?
explanation: Magnetization is equal to the net magnetic moment per unit volume. Alternatively {tex}\vec{{M}}=\frac{\vec{{m}}_{\text {net }}}{{V}}{/tex} Susceptibility is a measure of how a magnetic material responds to an external field. Alternatively {tex}\vec{{M}}=\chi \vec{{H}}{/tex} Susceptibility of paramagnetic material is between {tex}0 \& \varepsilon{/tex} (where {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} has small positive value) Alternatively {tex}{O} \lt \chi \lt 1{/tex} Susceptibility of diamagnetic material is between {tex}-1 \leq \chi \lt 0{/tex}
solution: Magnetization is equal to the net magnetic moment per unit volume. Alternatively {tex}\vec{{M}}=\frac{\vec{{m}}_{\text {net }}}{{V}}{/tex} Susceptibility is a measure of how a magnetic material responds to an external field. Alternatively {tex}\vec{{M}}=\chi \vec{{H}}{/tex} Susceptibility of paramagnetic material is between {tex}0 \& \varepsilon{/tex} (where {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} has small positive value) Alternatively {tex}{O} \lt \chi \lt 1{/tex} Susceptibility of diamagnetic material is between {tex}-1 \leq \chi \lt 0{/tex}
solution_html:
Magnetization is equal to the net magnetic moment per unit volume.
Alternatively
{tex}\vec{{M}}=\frac{\vec{{m}}_{\text {net }}}{{V}}{/tex}
Susceptibility is a measure of how a magnetic material responds to an external field.
Alternatively
{tex}\vec{{M}}=\chi \vec{{H}}{/tex}
Susceptibility of paramagnetic material is between {tex}0 \& \varepsilon{/tex} (where {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} has small positive value)
Alternatively
{tex}{O} \lt \chi \lt 1{/tex}
Susceptibility of diamagnetic material is between {tex}-1 \leq \chi \lt 0{/tex}

question_text: What are ferromagnetic materials? Explain ferromagnetism with the help of suitable diagrams, using the concept of magnetic domain.
explanation: Ferromagnetic substances are those which get strongly magnetised when placed in an external magnetic field. In absence of external magnetic field, domains are randomly oriented and it exhibits weak magnetisation. In the presence of external magnetic field domains orient themselves in the direction of magnetic field and it exhibits strong magnetisation.

  1. Do the magnetic field lines also represent the lines of force on a moving charged particle at every point?

question_text: Answer the following giving reasons: Do the magnetic field lines also represent the lines of force on a moving charged particle at every point? If magnetic monopoles existed, how would the Gauss's law of magnetism be modified?
explanation: No, The magnetic force is always normal to {tex}\vec {B}{/tex} . Gauss's law of magnetism states that the flux of {tex}\vec {B}{/tex} through any closed surface is always zero, {tex}\sum_\limits s \vec{B} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}=0{/tex} If monopoles existed, then Gauss' law {tex}\sum_\limits s \vec{B} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}\neq0{/tex}
solution: No, The magnetic force is always normal to {tex}\vec {B}{/tex} . Gauss's law of magnetism states that the flux of {tex}\vec {B}{/tex} through any closed surface is always zero, {tex}\sum_\limits s \vec{B} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}=0{/tex} If monopoles existed, then Gauss' law {tex}\sum_\limits s \vec{B} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}\neq0{/tex}
solution_html:
1. No, The magnetic force is always normal to {tex}\vec {B}{/tex}.
  2. Gauss's law of magnetism states that the flux of {tex}\vec {B}{/tex} through any closed surface is always zero, {tex}\sum_\limits s \vec{B} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}=0{/tex}
    If monopoles existed, then Gauss' law {tex}\sum_\limits s \vec{B} \cdot \Delta \vec{S}\neq0{/tex}

F = force applied at a distance 10 em from the centre
It is now in equilibrium at an angle = {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30o
External magnetic field strength = B = 0.25 T

Torque due to magnetic force = MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
= 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.25 {tex}\times{/tex} sin 30o

explanation: The equivalent magnetic moment is given by M = NiA The direction of p is perpendicular to the plane of the current-carrying loop. It is directed along the direction of advance of a right-handed screw rotated along the direction of flow of current Derivation of expression for M of an electron revolving around a nucleus For the loop, {tex}\vec{M}=N \pi r^{2} I \hat{k}{/tex} Magnetic potential energy = {tex}\bar{M} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{N} \pi r^{2} \mathrm{I} \hat{k}\left(B_{x} \hat{i}+B_{y} \hat{j}+B_{2} \hat{k}\right){/tex} Expression for the magnetic potential energy of the coil-magnetic field system.= {tex}\pi r^{2} N I B_{2}{/tex}

1. The equivalent magnetic moment is given by M = NiA
    The direction of p is perpendicular to the plane of the current-carrying loop. It is directed along the direction of advance of a right-handed screw rotated along the direction of flow of current Derivation of expression for M of an electron revolving around a nucleus
  2. For the loop, {tex}\vec{M}=N \pi r^{2} I \hat{k}{/tex}

     Expression for the magnetic potential energy of the coil-magnetic field system.= {tex}\pi r^{2} N I B_{2}{/tex}

  2. Write the four important properties of the magnetic field lines due to a bar magnet.
question_text: State Gauss’s law for magnetism. Explain its significance. Write the four important properties of the magnetic field lines due to a bar magnet.
explanation: Gauss’s law for magnetism states that “the total magnetic flux crossing through any closed surface is always zero". Mathematically, {tex}\oint\limits_s {\overrightarrow B } .\overrightarrow {ds}{/tex} = 0 The significance of Gauss's Law for magnetism is that the magnetic monopoles do not exists. The important properties of magnetic field lines are: Magnetic field lines always form continuous closed loops. These move from the north pole to the south pole outside the magnet and from south to north pole inside the magnet. The tangent drawn at a point on a magnetic field line at a given point gives the direction of the net magnetic field at that point. The larger number of field lines crossing per unit area represents the stronger magnetic field at that place. Magnetic field lines do not intersect each other.

    The significance of Gauss's Law for magnetism is that the magnetic monopoles do not exists.
  2. The important properties of magnetic field lines are:
    1. Magnetic field lines always form continuous closed loops. These move from the north pole to the south pole outside the magnet and from south to north pole inside the magnet.
    2. The tangent drawn at a point on a magnetic field line at a given point gives the direction of the net magnetic field at that point.
    3. The larger number of field lines crossing per unit area represents the stronger magnetic field at that place.
    4. Magnetic field lines do not intersect each other.

Four properties of magnetic field lines.

  1. Magnetic field lines always form continuous closed loops.

question_text: Show that the time period (T) of oscillations of a freely suspended magnetic dipole of magnetic moment (m) in a uniform magnetic field (B) is given by {tex}T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{m B}}{/tex} , where I is a moment of inertia of the magnetic dipole. Identify the following magnetic materials: A material having susceptibility ( {tex}\chi_{m}{/tex} ) = -0.00015. A material having susceptibility ( {tex}\chi_{m}{/tex} ) = 10 -5 .
explanation: Let us consider a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} exists in the region, in which a magnet of dipole moment {tex}\vec{m}{/tex} is placed. The dipole is making small angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the magnetic field. The torque acts on the magnet is given by {tex}\vec{\tau} =\vec{m} \times \vec{B}{/tex} = mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} In magnitude, {tex}\tau{/tex} = mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = -mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} in small) ...(i) Also the torque on dipole try to restore its initial position i.e., along the direction of magnetic field. (I = moment of inertia) In equilibrium {tex}I \frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-m B \sin \theta{/tex} ...(ii) Negative sign implies that restoring torque is in opposition to deflecting torque. {tex}\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=\frac{-m B}{I} \theta{/tex} ...(iii) Comparing with equation of angular SHM {tex}\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-\omega^2 \phi{/tex} ...(iv) We have {tex}\omega^2 =\frac{m B}{I} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{\frac{m B}{I}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 \pi}{T} =\sqrt{\frac{m B}{I}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{T}{2 \pi}=\sqrt{\frac{I}{m B}}{/tex} {tex}T =2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{m B}}{/tex} Diamagnetic substance. Paramagnetic substance.
solution: Let us consider a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} exists in the region, in which a magnet of dipole moment {tex}\vec{m}{/tex} is placed. The dipole is making small angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the magnetic field. The torque acts on the magnet is given by {tex}\vec{\tau} =\vec{m} \times \vec{B}{/tex} = mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} In magnitude, {tex}\tau{/tex} = mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = -mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} in small) ...(i) Also the torque on dipole try to restore its initial position i.e., along the direction of magnetic field. (I = moment of inertia) In equilibrium {tex}I \frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-m B \sin \theta{/tex} ...(ii) Negative sign implies that restoring torque is in opposition to deflecting torque. {tex}\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=\frac{-m B}{I} \theta{/tex} ...(iii) Comparing with equation of angular SHM {tex}\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-\omega^2 \phi{/tex} ...(iv) We have {tex}\omega^2 =\frac{m B}{I} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{\frac{m B}{I}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 \pi}{T} =\sqrt{\frac{m B}{I}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{T}{2 \pi}=\sqrt{\frac{I}{m B}}{/tex} {tex}T =2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{m B}}{/tex} Diamagnetic substance. Paramagnetic substance.
solution_html:
1. Let us consider a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} exists in the region, in which a magnet of dipole moment {tex}\vec{m}{/tex} is placed. The dipole is making small angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the magnetic field. The torque acts on the magnet is given by
    {tex}\vec{\tau} =\vec{m} \times \vec{B}{/tex}
    = mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} In magnitude, {tex}\tau{/tex} = mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
    = -mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} in small) ...(i)
    Also the torque on dipole try to restore its initial position i.e., along the direction of magnetic field. (I = moment of inertia)
    In equilibrium
    {tex}I \frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-m B \sin \theta{/tex} ...(ii)
    Negative sign implies that restoring torque is in opposition to deflecting torque.
    {tex}\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=\frac{-m B}{I} \theta{/tex} ...(iii)
    Comparing with equation of angular SHM
    {tex}\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-\omega^2 \phi{/tex} ...(iv)
    We have
    {tex}\omega^2 =\frac{m B}{I} {/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{\frac{m B}{I}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 \pi}{T} =\sqrt{\frac{m B}{I}}{/tex}{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{T}{2 \pi}=\sqrt{\frac{I}{m B}}{/tex}
    {tex}T =2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{m B}}{/tex}
    1. Diamagnetic substance.

question_text: A bar magnet of magnetic moment 6 J/T is aligned at 60 o with a uniform external magnetic field of 0.44 T. Calculate the work done in turning the magnet to align its magnetic moment normal to the magnetic field opposite to the magnetic field the torque on the magnet in the final orientation in case (ii).
explanation: When a dipole is rotated from 1 position to other position 2 in a uniform magnetic field, Workdone, W = mB(cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex} ) (i) Here {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 60 o , {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 90 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} Workdone, W = mB(cos 60 o - cos 90 o ) = mB( {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} - 0) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mB = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.44 = 1.32 J (ii) Here {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 60 o , {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 180 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} Workdone, W = mB(cos 60 o - cos 180 o ) = mB[ {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} - (-1)] = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} mB = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.44 = 3.96 J Torque, {tex}\tau{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{m} \times \overline{B}|{/tex} = mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} For {tex}\theta{/tex} = 180 o , we have Torque, {tex}\tau{/tex} = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.44 sin 180 o = 0

solution_html:
1. When a dipole is rotated from 1 position to other position 2 in a uniform magnetic field,
    Workdone, W = mB(cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex})
    (i) Here {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 60o, {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 90o 
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Workdone, W = mB(cos 60o - cos 90o)
    = mB({tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} - 0) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mB
    = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.44 = 1.32 J

    (ii) Here {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 60o, {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 180o 
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Workdone, W = mB(cos 60o - cos 180o)
    = mB[{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} - (-1)] = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}mB
    = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.44 = 3.96  J
                 
  2. Torque, {tex}\tau{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{m} \times \overline{B}|{/tex} = mB sin{tex}\theta{/tex} 
    For {tex}\theta{/tex} = 180o, we have
    Torque, {tex}\tau{/tex} = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.44 sin 180o = 0

{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\oint B d l=B \times 2 \pi r{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ienclosed = {tex}2 \pi r n \times I{/tex} 
From Ampere's circuital law, we have
{tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{o} \mu_{r} I_{\text {enclosed }}{/tex} 
{tex}B \times 2 \pi r=\mu_{n} \mu_{r} \times 2 \pi r n I{/tex} or B = {tex}\mu_{0} \mu_{r} n I{/tex}

question_html: From a molecular viewpoint, discuss the temperature dependence of susceptibility for diamagnetism, paramagnetism, and ferromagnetism.
question_text: From a molecular viewpoint, discuss the temperature dependence of susceptibility for diamagnetism, paramagnetism, and ferromagnetism.
explanation: The direction of external magnetic field H and magnetism M due to the orbital motion of electrons of diamagnetic substance are opposite so net magnetism becomes zero. Hence, the susceptibility ( {tex}\chi{/tex} ) of diamagnetism is not much affected by temperature. The direction of magnetism due to the orbital motion of electrons in paramagnetism and ferromagnetism material and externally applied field are in the same direction so net magnetism increased and much affected by temperature. As temperature raised the alignment of atomic magnetism is disturbed resulting in a decrease in susceptibility.

question_html: What do you mean by diamagnetism, paramagnetism and ferromagnetism?
question_text: What do you mean by diamagnetism, paramagnetism and ferromagnetism?
explanation: Diamagnetic Diamagnetic substances have a tendency to move from the stronger part to the weaker part of the external magnetic field. We can also say that the diamagnetic substances get repelled by a magnet. Paramagnetism Paramagnetic substances are those substances that get weakly magnetized in the presence of an external magnetic field. In the presence of an external magnetic field, these substances tend to move from a region of a weak to a strong magnetic field. In other terms, we can say that these substances tend to get weakly attracted to a permanent magnet. In a paramagnetic material, the individual atoms possess a dipole moment, which when placed in a magnetic field, interact with one another, and get spontaneously aligned in a common direction, which results in its magnetization. Ferromagnetism Ferromagnetic substances are those substances that when it’s placed in an external magnetic field, get strongly magnetized. Also, they tend to move from a region of weak to the region of a strong magnetic field and get strongly attracted to a magnet. In a ferromagnetic material, the individual atoms possess a dipole moment, similar to a paramagnetic material. When placed in a magnetic field, the atoms interact with one another and get spontaneously aligned in a common direction.
solution: Diamagnetic Diamagnetic substances have a tendency to move from the stronger part to the weaker part of the external magnetic field. We can also say that the diamagnetic substances get repelled by a magnet. Paramagnetism Paramagnetic substances are those substances that get weakly magnetized in the presence of an external magnetic field. In the presence of an external magnetic field, these substances tend to move from a region of a weak to a strong magnetic field. In other terms, we can say that these substances tend to get weakly attracted to a permanent magnet. In a paramagnetic material, the individual atoms possess a dipole moment, which when placed in a magnetic field, interact with one another, and get spontaneously aligned in a common direction, which results in its magnetization. Ferromagnetism Ferromagnetic substances are those substances that when it’s placed in an external magnetic field, get strongly magnetized. Also, they tend to move from a region of weak to the region of a strong magnetic field and get strongly attracted to a magnet. In a ferromagnetic material, the individual atoms possess a dipole moment, similar to a paramagnetic material. When placed in a magnetic field, the atoms interact with one another and get spontaneously aligned in a common direction.
solution_html:
Diamagnetic

Diamagnetic substances have a tendency to move from the stronger part to the weaker part of the external magnetic field. We can also say that the diamagnetic substances get repelled by a magnet.

Paramagnetism

Paramagnetic substances are those substances that get weakly magnetized in the presence of an external magnetic field. In the presence of an external magnetic field, these substances tend to move from a region of a weak to a strong magnetic field. In other terms, we can say that these substances tend to get weakly attracted to a permanent magnet. In a paramagnetic material, the individual atoms possess a dipole moment, which when placed in a magnetic field, interact with one another, and get spontaneously aligned in a common direction, which results in its magnetization.

Ferromagnetism

Ferromagnetic substances are those substances that when it’s placed in an external magnetic field, get strongly magnetized. Also, they tend to move from a region of weak to the region of a strong magnetic field and get strongly attracted to a magnet. In a ferromagnetic material, the individual atoms possess a dipole moment, similar to a paramagnetic material. When placed in a magnetic field, the atoms interact with one another and get spontaneously aligned in a common direction.

question_html:
1.  A small compass needle of magnetic moment m is free to turn about an axis perpendicular to the direction of uniform magnetic field B. The moment of inertia of the needle about the axis is I. The needle is slightly disturbed from its stable position and then released. Prove that it executes simple harmonic motion. Hence, deduce the expression for its time period.
  2. Prove that the potential energy of a magnet in a uniform magnetic field is {tex}U=-\vec{M} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex}.
question_text: A small compass needle of magnetic moment m is free to turn about an axis perpendicular to the direction of uniform magnetic field B . The moment of inertia of the needle about the axis is I . The needle is slightly disturbed from its stable position and then released. Prove that it executes simple harmonic motion. Hence, deduce the expression for its time period. Prove that the potential energy of a magnet in a uniform magnetic field is {tex}U=-\vec{M} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex} .
explanation: Let magnetic needle is suspended in the uniform external magnetic field B and making angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with it and is slightly disturbed to oscillatein this field. As Magnetic moment {tex}\vec M{/tex} is directed along the axisof needle, torque {tex}\tau{/tex} on the needle is {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{M} \times \vec{B}{/tex} = MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} We also know that {tex}\tau=I \alpha{/tex} Where I is moment of inertia of needle and {tex}\alpha{/tex} angular acceleration Angular acceleration, {tex}\alpha=\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\tau=I \frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-M B \sin \theta{/tex} (-ve sign implies that this torque is acting as restoring torque). Now if {tex}\theta{/tex} is small,then sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex} So, {tex}I \frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-M B \theta{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=\left(\frac{-M B}{I}\right) \theta{/tex} ...(2) This derived equation is similar to equation of simple harmonic motion, {tex}\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-\omega^2 \theta{/tex} where, {tex}\omega^2=\frac{M B}{I}{/tex} Hence, it is proved that it executes simple harmonic motion. We know, that {tex}\omega=\frac{2 \pi}{T}{/tex} where, T is period of oscillation. {tex}\therefore \sqrt{\frac{M B}{I}}=\frac{2 \pi}{T}{/tex} {tex}T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{M B}}{/tex} The magnetic potential energy {tex}=U=\int \tau d \theta{/tex} {tex}=\int M B \sin \theta d \theta{/tex} = -MB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}=-\vec{M} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex}
solution: Let magnetic needle is suspended in the uniform external magnetic field B and making angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with it and is slightly disturbed to oscillatein this field. As Magnetic moment {tex}\vec M{/tex} is directed along the axisof needle, torque {tex}\tau{/tex} on the needle is {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{M} \times \vec{B}{/tex} = MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} We also know that {tex}\tau=I \alpha{/tex} Where I is moment of inertia of needle and {tex}\alpha{/tex} angular acceleration Angular acceleration, {tex}\alpha=\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\tau=I \frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-M B \sin \theta{/tex} (-ve sign implies that this torque is acting as restoring torque). Now if {tex}\theta{/tex} is small,then sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex} So, {tex}I \frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-M B \theta{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=\left(\frac{-M B}{I}\right) \theta{/tex} ...(2) This derived equation is similar to equation of simple harmonic motion, {tex}\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-\omega^2 \theta{/tex} where, {tex}\omega^2=\frac{M B}{I}{/tex} Hence, it is proved that it executes simple harmonic motion. We know, that {tex}\omega=\frac{2 \pi}{T}{/tex} where, T is period of oscillation. {tex}\therefore \sqrt{\frac{M B}{I}}=\frac{2 \pi}{T}{/tex} {tex}T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{M B}}{/tex} The magnetic potential energy {tex}=U=\int \tau d \theta{/tex} {tex}=\int M B \sin \theta d \theta{/tex} = -MB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}=-\vec{M} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Let magnetic needle is suspended in the uniform external magnetic field B and making angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with it and is slightly disturbed to oscillatein this field.
    As Magnetic moment {tex}\vec M{/tex} is directed along the axisof needle, torque {tex}\tau{/tex} on the needle is
    {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{M} \times \vec{B}{/tex}
    = MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
    We also know that 
    {tex}\tau=I \alpha{/tex}
    Where I is moment of inertia of needle and {tex}\alpha{/tex} angular acceleration
    Angular acceleration,
    {tex}\alpha=\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}{/tex}
    Hence, {tex}\tau=I \frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-M B \sin \theta{/tex}
    (-ve sign implies that this torque is acting as restoring torque).
    Now if {tex}\theta{/tex} is small,then sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex}
    So, {tex}I \frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-M B \theta{/tex}
    or,  {tex}\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=\left(\frac{-M B}{I}\right) \theta{/tex} ...(2)
    This derived equation is similar to equation of simple harmonic motion,
    {tex}\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-\omega^2 \theta{/tex}
    where, {tex}\omega^2=\frac{M B}{I}{/tex}
    Hence, it is proved that it executes simple harmonic motion.
    We know, that {tex}\omega=\frac{2 \pi}{T}{/tex}
    where, T is period of oscillation. 
    {tex}\therefore \sqrt{\frac{M B}{I}}=\frac{2 \pi}{T}{/tex}
    {tex}T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{M B}}{/tex}
  2. The magnetic potential energy
    {tex}=U=\int \tau d \theta{/tex}
    {tex}=\int M B \sin \theta d \theta{/tex}
    = -MB cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
    {tex}=-\vec{M} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex}

question_html:
1. How does a diamagnetic material behave when it is cooled at very low temperature?
  2. Why does a paramagnetic sample display greater magnetisation when cooled? Explain.
question_text: How does a diamagnetic material behave when it is cooled at very low temperature? Why does a paramagnetic sample display greater magnetisation when cooled? Explain.
explanation: For diamagnetic substances, magnetic susceptibility is negative, {tex}i.e. \chi _ { m } < 0{/tex} . These materials don't obey Curie's law. That's why diamagnetic materials are unaffected by the change in temperature (except bismuth). According to Curie's law, the magnetic susceptibility of a paramagnetic material is inversely proportional to the temperature. Hence, paramagnetic materials when cooled due to thermal agitation, tendency alignment of magnetic dipoles decreases. Hence, they show greater magnetisation.

According to Curie's law, the magnetic susceptibility of a paramagnetic material is inversely proportional to the temperature. Hence, paramagnetic materials when cooled due to thermal agitation, tendency alignment of magnetic dipoles decreases. Hence, they show greater magnetisation.

1. For diamagnetic substances, magnetic susceptibility is negative, {tex}i.e. \chi _ { m } < 0{/tex}. These materials don't obey Curie's law. That's why diamagnetic materials are unaffected by the change in temperature (except bismuth). 
  2. According to Curie's law, the magnetic susceptibility of a paramagnetic material is inversely proportional to the temperature. Hence, paramagnetic materials when cooled due to thermal agitation, tendency alignment of magnetic dipoles decreases. Hence, they show greater magnetisation.

question_html:
A short bar magnet of magnetic moment 0.9 JT-1 is placed with its axis at 30o to a uniform magnetic field. It experiences a torque of 0.063 J. 

  1. Calculate the magnitude of the magnetic field.
  2. In which orientation will the bar magnet be in stable equilibrium in the magnetic field?
question_text: A short bar magnet of magnetic moment 0.9 JT -1 is placed with its axis at 30 o to a uniform magnetic field. It experiences a torque of 0.063 J. Calculate the magnitude of the magnetic field. In which orientation will the bar magnet be in stable equilibrium in the magnetic field?
explanation: Magnetic moment M = 0.9 J/T {tex}\tau{/tex} = 0.063 J, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o We know {tex}\tau=M \times B{/tex} = MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} 0.063 = 0.9 {tex}\times{/tex} B {tex}\times{/tex} sin 30 o B = {tex}\frac{2 \times 0.063}{0.9}{/tex} = 0.14 T Stable equilibrium is position of minimum energy. Since {tex}U=-\vec{M} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex} U = - MB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} Where U is the energy stored or P.E. of the magnet inside magnetic field B. So, when {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0, U = -MB is the minimum energy. Thus, when {tex}\vec M{/tex} and {tex}\vec B{/tex} are parallel to each other bar magnet is in stable equilibrium.
solution: Magnetic moment M = 0.9 J/T {tex}\tau{/tex} = 0.063 J, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o We know {tex}\tau=M \times B{/tex} = MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} 0.063 = 0.9 {tex}\times{/tex} B {tex}\times{/tex} sin 30 o B = {tex}\frac{2 \times 0.063}{0.9}{/tex} = 0.14 T Stable equilibrium is position of minimum energy. Since {tex}U=-\vec{M} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex} U = - MB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} Where U is the energy stored or P.E. of the magnet inside magnetic field B. So, when {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0, U = -MB is the minimum energy. Thus, when {tex}\vec M{/tex} and {tex}\vec B{/tex} are parallel to each other bar magnet is in stable equilibrium.
solution_html:
1. Magnetic moment M = 0.9 J/T
    {tex}\tau{/tex} = 0.063 J, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30o 
    We know {tex}\tau=M \times B{/tex} 
    = MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} 
    0.063 = 0.9 {tex}\times{/tex} B {tex}\times{/tex} sin 30o 
    B = {tex}\frac{2 \times 0.063}{0.9}{/tex} = 0.14 T
  2. Stable equilibrium is position of minimum energy. Since {tex}U=-\vec{M} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex} 
    U = - MB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} 
    Where U is the energy stored or P.E. of the magnet inside magnetic field B.
    So, when {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0, U = -MB is the minimum energy.
    Thus, when {tex}\vec M{/tex} and {tex}\vec B{/tex} are parallel to each other bar magnet is in stable equilibrium.

question_html:
Explain the following:

  1. Why do magnetic lines of force form continuous closed loops?
  2. Why are the field lines repelled (expelled) when a diamagnetic material is placed in an external uniform magnetic field?
question_text: Explain the following: Why do magnetic lines of force form continuous closed loops? Why are the field lines repelled (expelled) when a diamagnetic material is placed in an external uniform magnetic field?
explanation: Outside the magnet, the magnetic lines starts from North pole and enter into the South pole whereas inside the magnet, the field lines travel from South pole to North pole. As monopoles do not exist, so, north and south pole always occur together. So, magnetic lines of force always form a closed loop. Due to different rotations of electrons, the diamagnetic materials is magnetized in the direction opposite to the external field, therefore lines of force are repelled by a diamagnetic material.
solution: Outside the magnet, the magnetic lines starts from North pole and enter into the South pole whereas inside the magnet, the field lines travel from South pole to North pole. As monopoles do not exist, so, north and south pole always occur together. So, magnetic lines of force always form a closed loop. Due to different rotations of electrons, the diamagnetic materials is magnetized in the direction opposite to the external field, therefore lines of force are repelled by a diamagnetic material.
solution_html:
1. Outside the magnet, the magnetic lines starts from North pole and enter into the South pole whereas inside the magnet, the field lines travel from South pole to North pole. As monopoles do not exist, so, north and south pole always occur together. So, magnetic lines of force always form a closed loop.

  2. Due to different rotations of electrons, the diamagnetic materials is magnetized in the direction opposite to the external field, therefore lines of force are repelled by a diamagnetic material.

  1. susceptibility and
  2. their behaviour in a non-uniform magnetic field
question_text: Distinguish between diamagnetic and ferromagnetic materials in terms of susceptibility and their behaviour in a non-uniform magnetic field
explanation: Susceptibility for diamagnetic material: It is independent of magnetic field and temperature (except for bismuth at low temperature). Susceptibility for ferromagnetic material: The susceptibility of ferromagnetic materials decreases steadily with increase in temperature. At the Curie temperature, the ferromagnetic materials become paramagnetic. Behaviour in non-uniform magnetic field: Diamagnetic substances are feebly repelled, whereas ferromagnets are strongly attracted by non-uniform field.
solution: Susceptibility for diamagnetic material: It is independent of magnetic field and temperature (except for bismuth at low temperature). Susceptibility for ferromagnetic material: The susceptibility of ferromagnetic materials decreases steadily with increase in temperature. At the Curie temperature, the ferromagnetic materials become paramagnetic. Behaviour in non-uniform magnetic field: Diamagnetic substances are feebly repelled, whereas ferromagnets are strongly attracted by non-uniform field.

1. Susceptibility for diamagnetic material:
    It is independent of magnetic field and temperature (except for bismuth at low temperature).
    Susceptibility for ferromagnetic material:
    The susceptibility of ferromagnetic materials decreases steadily with increase in temperature. At the Curie temperature, the ferromagnetic materials become paramagnetic. 
  2. Behaviour in non-uniform magnetic field:
    Diamagnetic substances are feebly repelled, whereas ferromagnets are strongly attracted by non-uniform field.

question_text: A small compass needle of magnetic moment M and moment of inertia I is free to oscillate in a magnetic field B. It is slightly disturbed from its equilibrium position and then released. Show that it executes simple harmonic motion. Write the expression for its time period.
explanation: When compass needle of magnetic moment M and moment of inertia I is slightly disturbed by an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} from the mean position of equilibrium. Then, restoring torque begins to act on the needle which try to bring the needle back to its mean position which is given by {tex}\vec {\tau} = - \vec M× \vec B{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tau = - M B \sin \theta{/tex} (taking only magnitude), B being magnetic field intensity. Since, {tex}\theta {/tex} is small, so, {tex}\sin \theta = \theta{/tex} {tex}\tau = - M B \theta{/tex} ...... (i) But {tex}\tau = I \alpha{/tex} ...... (ii) where, {tex}\alpha {/tex} = angular acceleration, I = moment of inertia of the needle and M = magnetic moment of dipole On comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii), {tex}\Rightarrow I \alpha = - M B \theta \Rightarrow \alpha = - ( M B / I ) \theta {/tex} .....(iii) Thus, angular acceleration {tex}\alpha {/tex} is directly proportional to angular displacement {tex}\theta{/tex} . Therefore, the needle will execute SHM. Comparing equation (iii) with the general form of SHM, {tex}\alpha = - {\omega ^2}\theta{/tex} , we get {tex}\omega = \sqrt {\frac {MB}{I}}{/tex} Hence, the time period, {tex}T = \frac { 2 \pi } { \omega } = \frac { 2 \pi } { \sqrt { M B / I } } \text { or } \quad T = 2 \pi \sqrt { \frac { I } { M B } }{/tex} This is the required expression.
solution: When compass needle of magnetic moment M and moment of inertia I is slightly disturbed by an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} from the mean position of equilibrium. Then, restoring torque begins to act on the needle which try to bring the needle back to its mean position which is given by {tex}\vec {\tau} = - \vec M× \vec B{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tau = - M B \sin \theta{/tex} (taking only magnitude), B being magnetic field intensity. Since, {tex}\theta {/tex} is small, so, {tex}\sin \theta = \theta{/tex} {tex}\tau = - M B \theta{/tex} ...... (i) But {tex}\tau = I \alpha{/tex} ...... (ii) where, {tex}\alpha {/tex} = angular acceleration, I = moment of inertia of the needle and M = magnetic moment of dipole On comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii), {tex}\Rightarrow I \alpha = - M B \theta \Rightarrow \alpha = - ( M B / I ) \theta {/tex} .....(iii) Thus, angular acceleration {tex}\alpha {/tex} is directly proportional to angular displacement {tex}\theta{/tex} . Therefore, the needle will execute SHM. Comparing equation (iii) with the general form of SHM, {tex}\alpha = - {\omega ^2}\theta{/tex} , we get {tex}\omega = \sqrt {\frac {MB}{I}}{/tex} Hence, the time period, {tex}T = \frac { 2 \pi } { \omega } = \frac { 2 \pi } { \sqrt { M B / I } } \text { or } \quad T = 2 \pi \sqrt { \frac { I } { M B } }{/tex} This is the required expression.
solution_html:
When compass needle of magnetic moment M and moment of inertia I is slightly disturbed by an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} from the mean position of equilibrium. Then, restoring torque begins to act on the needle which try to bring the needle back to its mean position which is given by
{tex}\vec {\tau} = - \vec M× \vec B{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tau = - M B \sin \theta{/tex} (taking only magnitude), B being magnetic field intensity.
Since, {tex}\theta {/tex} is small, so, {tex}\sin \theta = \theta{/tex}
{tex}\tau = - M B \theta{/tex} ...... (i)
But {tex}\tau = I \alpha{/tex} ...... (ii)
where, {tex}\alpha {/tex} = angular acceleration, I = moment of inertia of the needle and M = magnetic moment of dipole
On comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii),
{tex}\Rightarrow I \alpha = - M B \theta \Rightarrow \alpha = - ( M B / I ) \theta {/tex} .....(iii)
Thus, angular acceleration {tex}\alpha {/tex} is directly proportional to angular displacement {tex}\theta{/tex}. Therefore, the needle will execute SHM.
Comparing equation (iii) with the general form of SHM, {tex}\alpha = - {\omega ^2}\theta{/tex}, we get
{tex}\omega = \sqrt {\frac {MB}{I}}{/tex}
Hence, the time period,
{tex}T = \frac { 2 \pi } { \omega } = \frac { 2 \pi } { \sqrt { M B / I } } \text { or } \quad T = 2 \pi \sqrt { \frac { I } { M B } }{/tex}
This is the required expression.

question_html: Two magnets of magnetic moment M and M{tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} are joined to form a cross. The combination is suspended in a uniform magnetic field B. The magnetic moment now makes an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the field direction. Find the value of the angle {tex}\theta{/tex}.
question_text: Two magnets of magnetic moment M and M {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} are joined to form a cross. The combination is suspended in a uniform magnetic field B. The magnetic moment now makes an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the field direction. Find the value of the angle {tex}\theta{/tex} .
explanation: In equilibrium condition, torque on both magnets are equal and opposite. Hence, MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} M sin (90° - {tex}\theta{/tex} ) MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} M cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ,by rearranging the equation we get, {tex}\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{M}}{/tex} tan {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} or {tex}\theta{/tex} = 60°
solution: In equilibrium condition, torque on both magnets are equal and opposite. Hence, MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} M sin (90° - {tex}\theta{/tex} ) MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} M cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ,by rearranging the equation we get, {tex}\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{M}}{/tex} tan {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} or {tex}\theta{/tex} = 60°
solution_html:
In equilibrium condition, torque on both magnets are equal and opposite. Hence,
MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} M sin (90° - {tex}\theta{/tex})
MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} M cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ,by rearranging the equation we get,
{tex}\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{M}}{/tex}
tan {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} or {tex}\theta{/tex} = 60°

question_html: Using the relation for the potential energy of a current-carrying planar loop, in a uniform magnetic field, obtain the expression for the work done in moving the planar loop from its unstable (equilibrium) position.
question_text: Using the relation for the potential energy of a current-carrying planar loop, in a uniform magnetic field, obtain the expression for the work done in moving the planar loop from its unstable (equilibrium) position.
explanation: P.E. of current loop when its dipole moment {tex}\vec m{/tex} makes angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the field {tex}\vec B{/tex} is given by U = -mB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} In unstable equilibrium position, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 180 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} U 1 = -mB cos 180 o = +mB In stable equilibrium position, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} U 2 = -mB cos 0 o = -mB Required work, W = U 2 - U 1 = -mB - mB = -2mB = -2IAB [ {tex}\because{/tex} m = IA]
solution: P.E. of current loop when its dipole moment {tex}\vec m{/tex} makes angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the field {tex}\vec B{/tex} is given by U = -mB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} In unstable equilibrium position, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 180 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} U 1 = -mB cos 180 o = +mB In stable equilibrium position, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} U 2 = -mB cos 0 o = -mB Required work, W = U 2 - U 1 = -mB - mB = -2mB = -2IAB [ {tex}\because{/tex} m = IA]
solution_html:
P.E. of current loop when its dipole moment {tex}\vec m{/tex} makes angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the field {tex}\vec B{/tex} is given by
U = -mB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} 
In unstable equilibrium position, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 180o 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} U1 = -mB cos 180o = +mB
In stable equilibrium position, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0o 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} U2 = -mB cos 0o = -mB
Required work,
W = U2 - U1 = -mB - mB
= -2mB = -2IAB [{tex}\because{/tex} m = IA]

question_text: When two materials are placed in an external magnetic field, the behaviour of magnetic field lines is as shown in the figure. Identify the magnetic nature of each of these two materials.
explanation: Material X is a paramagnetic substance. When a specimen of a paramagnetic substance is placed in a magnetising field, the lines of force are feebly attracted by the material and for that reason, only a few of them prefer to pass through the specimen rather than through air. Material Y is a ferromagnetic substance. These are the substances in which a strong magnetism is produced in the same direction as the applied magnetic field. Magnetic field lines are strongly attracted by these kinds of materials. For that reason, most of the lines prefer to move through these materials which exhibit highly concentrated lines of force.
solution: Material X is a paramagnetic substance. When a specimen of a paramagnetic substance is placed in a magnetising field, the lines of force are feebly attracted by the material and for that reason, only a few of them prefer to pass through the specimen rather than through air. Material Y is a ferromagnetic substance. These are the substances in which a strong magnetism is produced in the same direction as the applied magnetic field. Magnetic field lines are strongly attracted by these kinds of materials. For that reason, most of the lines prefer to move through these materials which exhibit highly concentrated lines of force.
solution_html:
Material X is a paramagnetic substance. When a specimen of a paramagnetic substance is placed in a magnetising field, the lines of force are feebly attracted by the material and for that reason, only a few of them prefer to pass through the specimen rather than through air. 

Material Y is a ferromagnetic substance. These are the substances in which a strong magnetism is produced in the same direction as the applied magnetic field. Magnetic field lines are strongly attracted by these kinds of materials. For that reason, most of the lines prefer to move through these materials which exhibit highly concentrated lines of force.

question_text: A magnetising field of 1500 A/m produces a magnetic flux of 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 weber in a bar of iron of crosssection 0.5 cm 2 . Calculate permeability and susceptibility of the iron-bar used.
explanation: Here H = 1500 Am -1 , {tex}\phi{/tex} = 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 Wb, A = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m 2 Magnetic induction, B = {tex}\frac{\phi}{A}=\frac{2.4 \times 10^{-5}}{0.5 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} = 0.48 Wbm -2 Permeability, {tex}\mu=\frac{B}{H}=\frac{0.48}{1500}{/tex} = 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 TmA -1 As {tex}\mu=\mu_{0}\left(1+\chi_{m}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Susceptibility, {tex}\chi_{m}=\frac{\mu}{\mu_{0}}-1=\frac{3.2 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-7}}-1{/tex} = 254.77 - 1 = 253.77
solution: Here H = 1500 Am -1 , {tex}\phi{/tex} = 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 Wb, A = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m 2 Magnetic induction, B = {tex}\frac{\phi}{A}=\frac{2.4 \times 10^{-5}}{0.5 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} = 0.48 Wbm -2 Permeability, {tex}\mu=\frac{B}{H}=\frac{0.48}{1500}{/tex} = 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 TmA -1 As {tex}\mu=\mu_{0}\left(1+\chi_{m}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Susceptibility, {tex}\chi_{m}=\frac{\mu}{\mu_{0}}-1=\frac{3.2 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-7}}-1{/tex} = 254.77 - 1 = 253.77

Here H = 1500 Am-1, {tex}\phi{/tex} = 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 Wb, A = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 m2 
Magnetic induction,
B = {tex}\frac{\phi}{A}=\frac{2.4 \times 10^{-5}}{0.5 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} = 0.48 Wbm-2 
Permeability,
{tex}\mu=\frac{B}{H}=\frac{0.48}{1500}{/tex} = 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 TmA-1 
As {tex}\mu=\mu_{0}\left(1+\chi_{m}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Susceptibility,
{tex}\chi_{m}=\frac{\mu}{\mu_{0}}-1=\frac{3.2 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-7}}-1{/tex} 
= 254.77 - 1 = 253.77

question_text: A frog can be levitated in a magnetic field produced by a current in a vertical solenoid placed below the frog. This is possible, because the body of the frog behaves as

options.explanation: To levitate a body, a force must be applied on it which at least balances the body's weight. Since weight will always pull the frog down, the magnetic force on the frog due to the vertical solenoid placed below it must act in the upward direction. Thus, the frog is repelled by the magnetic field. Diamagnetic substances are the only substances which are repelled by a magnetic field. This shows that the body of the frog behaves diamagnetically.

question_html:
A bar magnet is placed in a uniform magnetic field with its magnetic moment making an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the field.

  1. Write an expression for the torque acting on the magnet and hence define its magnetic moment.
  2. Write an expression for the potential energy of the magnet in this orientation. When is this energy minimum?
question_text: A bar magnet is placed in a uniform magnetic field with its magnetic moment making an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the field. Write an expression for the torque acting on the magnet and hence define its magnetic moment. Write an expression for the potential energy of the magnet in this orientation. When is this energy minimum?
explanation: The torque acting on a bar magnet of magnetic moment {tex}\vec m{/tex} placed in magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} at an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with it is given by {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B}{/tex} Magnitude of torque, {tex}\tau=m B \sin \theta{/tex} If B = 1, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90 o , sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1, then {tex}\tau{/tex} = m Hence magnetic moment may be defined as the torque acting on a magnetic dipole placed perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of unit strength. Potential energy, U = -mB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} When {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o , U = -mB cos 0 o = -mB {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = -mB Thus the potential energy of the magnet is minimum when its magnetic moment {tex}\vec m{/tex} is aligned parallel to the field {tex}\vec B{/tex} .
solution: The torque acting on a bar magnet of magnetic moment {tex}\vec m{/tex} placed in magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} at an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with it is given by {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B}{/tex} Magnitude of torque, {tex}\tau=m B \sin \theta{/tex} If B = 1, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90 o , sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1, then {tex}\tau{/tex} = m Hence magnetic moment may be defined as the torque acting on a magnetic dipole placed perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of unit strength. Potential energy, U = -mB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} When {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o , U = -mB cos 0 o = -mB {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = -mB Thus the potential energy of the magnet is minimum when its magnetic moment {tex}\vec m{/tex} is aligned parallel to the field {tex}\vec B{/tex} .
solution_html:
1. The torque acting on a bar magnet of magnetic moment {tex}\vec m{/tex} placed in magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} at an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with it is given by {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B}{/tex} 
    Magnitude of torque, {tex}\tau=m B \sin \theta{/tex} 
    If B = 1, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90o, sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1, then {tex}\tau{/tex} = m
    Hence magnetic moment may be defined as the torque acting on a magnetic dipole placed perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of unit strength.
  2. Potential energy, U = -mB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} 
    When {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0o, U = -mB cos 0o = -mB {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = -mB
    Thus the potential energy of the magnet is minimum when its magnetic moment {tex}\vec m{/tex} is aligned parallel to the field {tex}\vec B{/tex}.

question_text: Derive an expression for the torque experienced by a magnetic dipole in a uniform magnetic field. Hence obtain the expression for the potential energy of the dipole.
explanation: Consider a bar magnet NS of length 2l placed in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} as shown in the figure. Let q m be the pole strength of its every pole. Let the magnetic axis of the bar magnet make an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the field {tex}\vec B{/tex} , as shown in the figure. Magnetic force on N-pole = q m B ; along {tex}\vec B{/tex} Magnetic force on S-pole = q m B, opposite to {tex}\vec B{/tex} The forces on the two poles are equal and opposite hence they form a couple. Moment of couple or torque is given by {tex}\tau{/tex} = Force {tex}\times{/tex} perpendicular distance = {tex}q_{m} B \times 2 l \sin \theta{/tex} = {tex}\left(q_{m} \times 2 l\right) B \sin \theta{/tex} or {tex}\tau{/tex} = mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} .....(i) where m = q m {tex}\times{/tex} 2l, is the magnetic dipole moment of the bar magnet. In vector notation, {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B}{/tex} As shown in figure, When a magnetic dipole is placed in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} at angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with it, it experiences a torque {tex}\tau{/tex} = mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} This torque tends to align the dipole in the direction of magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} . The work done in turning the dipole through a small angle d {tex}\theta{/tex} is dW = {tex}\tau d \theta=m B \sin \theta d \theta{/tex} If the dipole is rotated from an initial position {tex}\theta=\theta_{1}{/tex} to the final position {tex}\theta=\theta_{2}{/tex} then the total work done will be W = {tex}\int d W=\int_{\theta_{1}}^{\theta_{2}} m B \sin \theta d \theta=m B[-\cos \theta]_{\theta_{1}}^{\theta_{2}}{/tex} = -mB(cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} ) This work done is stored as the potential energy U of the dipole. U = -mB(cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} ) The potential energy of the dipole is zero when {tex}\vec{m} \perp \vec{B}{/tex} . So potential energy of the dipole in any orientation {tex}\theta{/tex} can be obtained by putting {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 90° and {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex} in the above equation. U = -mB(cos {tex}\theta{/tex} - cos 90 o ) or U = {tex}-m B \cos \theta=-\vec{m} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex}

= {tex}q_{m} B \times 2 l \sin \theta{/tex} = {tex}\left(q_{m} \times 2 l\right) B \sin \theta{/tex}

When a magnetic dipole is placed in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} at angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with it, it experiences a torque
{tex}\tau{/tex} = mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} 
This torque tends to align the dipole in the direction of magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex}.
The work done in turning the dipole through a small angle d{tex}\theta{/tex} is
dW = {tex}\tau d \theta=m B \sin \theta d \theta{/tex} 
If the dipole is rotated from an initial position {tex}\theta=\theta_{1}{/tex} to the final position {tex}\theta=\theta_{2}{/tex} then the total work done will be
W = {tex}\int d W=\int_{\theta_{1}}^{\theta_{2}} m B \sin \theta d \theta=m B[-\cos \theta]_{\theta_{1}}^{\theta_{2}}{/tex} 
= -mB(cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex})

question_html: State four basic properties of magnets.
question_text: State four basic properties of magnets.
explanation: Basic properties of magnets. These are as follows: Attractive property: A magnet attracts small pieces of iron, nickel, cobalt, etc. Directive property: A freely suspended magnet aligns itself nearly in the geographic north-south direction. Like poles repel and unlike poles attract: This is a fundamental law of magnetic poles. Magnetic poles exist in pairs: Isolated magnetic poles do not exist. If we break a magnet into two pieces, we get two smaller dipole magnets.

Basic properties of magnets. These are as follows:

  1. Attractive property: A magnet attracts small pieces of iron, nickel, cobalt, etc.
  2. Directive property: A freely suspended magnet aligns itself nearly in the geographic north-south direction.
  3. Like poles repel and unlike poles attract: This is a fundamental law of magnetic poles.
  4. Magnetic poles exist in pairs: Isolated magnetic poles do not exist. If we break a magnet into two pieces, we get two smaller dipole magnets.

A short bar magnet placed with its axis at 30° to a uniform magnetic field of 0.2 T experiences a torque of 0.06 Nm.

  1. Calculate the magnetic moment of the magnet,
  2. Find out what orientation of the magnet corresponds to its stable equilibrium in the magnetic field.
question_text: A short bar magnet placed with its axis at 30° to a uniform magnetic field of 0.2 T experiences a torque of 0.06 Nm. Calculate the magnetic moment of the magnet, Find out what orientation of the magnet corresponds to its stable equilibrium in the magnetic field.
explanation: Here B = 0.2 T, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o , {tex}\tau{/tex} = 0.06 Nm Magnetic moment, m = {tex}\frac{\tau}{B \sin \theta}=\frac{0.06}{0.2 \sin 30^{\circ}}=\frac{0.06}{0.2 \times 0.5}{/tex} = 0.6 Am 2 The P.E. of a magnetic dipole in a uniform magnetic field is U = -mB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} In stable equilibrium, the P.E. is minimum. So, cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 or {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o Hence the bar magnet will be in stable equilibrium when its magnetic moment {tex}\vec m{/tex} is parallel to the magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} .

explanation: Diamagnetic substances: Diamagnetic substances are those which develop feeble magnetisation in the opposite direction of the magnetising field. Such substances are feebly repelled by magnets and tend to move from stronger to weaker parts of a magnetic field. Example: Bismuth Paramagnetic substances: Paramagnetic substances are those which develop feeble magnetisation in the direction of the magnetising field. Such substances are feebly attracted by magnets and tend to move from weaker to stronger parts of a magnetic field. Example: Manganese Ferromagnetic substances: Ferromagnetic substances are those which develop strong magnetisation in the direction of the magnetising field. They are strongly attracted by magnets and tend to move from weaker to stronger parts of a magnetic field. Example: Iron

question_html: Draw a hysteresis loop with axes labelled and showing remanence and coercive force. What does the area of the loop represent?
question_text: Draw a hysteresis loop with axes labelled and showing remanence and coercive force. What does the area of the loop represent?
explanation: Hysteresis occurs in a system that involves a magnetic field. Hysteresis is the common property of ferromagnetic substances. Generally, when the magnetization of ferromagnetic materials lags behind the magnetic field this effect can be described as the hysteresis effect. Definition: The meaning of hysteresis is ”lagging”. Hysteresis is characterized as a lag of magnetization intensity (B) behind the magnetic field intensity (H). All ferromagnetic materials exhibit the phenomena of hysteresis. To give you a better understanding of the concept, we will take an instance where a ferromagnetic substance is placed inside a current-carrying coil. Due to the magnetic field that is present the substance gets magnetized. If we reverse the direction of current then the substance gets demagnetized, this process is known as hysteresis. There are two types of hysteresis; Rate-dependent hysteresis rate-independent hysteresis Hysteresis Loop The hysteresis loop shows the relationship between the intensity of magnetization and the magnetizing field. The loop is generated by measuring the magnetic flux coming out from the ferromagnetic substance while changing the external magnetizing field. Looking at the graph, if B is measured for various values of H and if the results are plotted in graphic forms then the graph will show a hysteresis loop. The intensity of the magnetism (B) is increased when the magnetic field (H) is increased from 0 (zero). With increasing the magnetic field there is an increase in the value of magnetism and finally reaches point A which is called saturation point where B is constant. With a decrease in the value of the magnetic field, there is a decrease in the value of magnetism. But at B and H are equal to zero, substance or material retains some amount of magnetism is called retentivity or residual magnetism. When there is a decrease in the magnetic field towards the negative side, magnetism also decreases. At point C the substance is completely demagnetized. The force required to remove the retentivity of the material is known as Coercive force (C). In the opposite direction, the cycle is continued where the saturation point is D, retentivity point is E and coercive force is F. Due to the forward and opposite direction process, the cycle is complete and this cycle is called the hysteresis loop.

  2. rate-independent hysteresis

Hysteresis Loop
The hysteresis loop shows the relationship between the intensity of magnetization and the magnetizing field. The loop is generated by measuring the magnetic flux coming out from the ferromagnetic substance while changing the external magnetizing field.

  2. With increasing the magnetic field there is an increase in the value of magnetism and finally reaches point A which is called saturation point where B is constant.

  4. When there is a decrease in the magnetic field towards the negative side, magnetism also decreases. At point C the substance is completely demagnetized.
  5. The force required to remove the retentivity of the material is known as Coercive force (C).
  6. In the opposite direction, the cycle is continued where the saturation point is D, retentivity point is E and coercive force is F.
  7. Due to the forward and opposite direction process, the cycle is complete and this cycle is called the hysteresis loop.

question_html: A magnetised needle in a uniform magnetic field experiences a torque but no net force. An iron nail near a bar magnet, however, experiences a force of attraction in addition to a torque. Why?
question_text: A magnetised needle in a uniform magnetic field experiences a torque but no net force. An iron nail near a bar magnet, however, experiences a force of attraction in addition to a torque. Why?
explanation: The force and torque act on the nail due to the induced magnetic moment acquired by it. The iron needle will also not experience any force, if magnetic field is uniform. The magnetic field due to a bar magnet is not uniform. Therefore, an iron nail experiences both a force and torque, when placed near a bar magnet. It may be pointed that the nail experiences a net attractive force. It is because the attractive force on the nearer end (unlike induced pole) of the nail is greater than the repulsive force on its farther end (like induced pole).

question_html: Can two magnetic lines of force intersect? Justify your answer.
question_text: Can two magnetic lines of force intersect? Justify your answer.
explanation: No. If the two magnetic lines of force intersect, then there will be two tangents and hence two directions of the magnetic field at the point of intersection. This is impossible.

question_html: Derive an expression for the magnetic field due to a magnetic dipole in broad-side on position at a distance r from its centre. The length of the dipole is 2l and its magnetic moment is m.
question_text: Derive an expression for the magnetic field due to a magnetic dipole in broad-side on position at a distance r from its centre. The length of the dipole is 2l and its magnetic moment is m.
explanation: Consider a bar magnet NS of length 2l and of pole strength q m . Suppose the magnetic field is to be determined at a point P lying on the equatorial line of the magnet NS at a distance r from its centre, as shown in the figure. Imagine a unit north-pole placed at point P. Then from Coulomb's law of magnetic forces, the force exerted by the N-pole of the magnet on unit north-pole is F N = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{q_{m}}{x^{2}}{/tex} , along NP Similarly, the force exerted by the S-pole of the magnet on unit north-pole is F S = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{q_{m}}{x^{2}}{/tex} , along PS As the magnitudes of F N and F S are equal, so their vertical components get cancelled while the horizontal components add up along PR. Hence the magnetic field at the equatorial point P is B equa = Net force on a unit N-pole placed at point P = F N cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + F S cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 2 F N cos {tex}\theta{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} F N = F S ] = {tex}2 \cdot \frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{q_{m}}{x^{2}} \cdot \frac{l}{x}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} cos {tex}\theta=\frac{l}{x}{/tex} ] or B equa = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{m}{\left(r^{2}+l^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} x = (r 2 + l 2 ) 1/2 ] where m = q m .2l, is the magnetic dipole moment. Again for a short magnet, l << r, so we have B equa = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{m}{r^{3}}{/tex} , along PR Clearly, the magnetic field at any equatorial point of a magnetic dipole is in the direction opposite to that of its magnetic dipole moment i.e., from N-pole to S-pole. So we can write {tex}\vec{B}_{\text {equa }}=-\frac{\mu_{0} \vec{m}}{4 \pi r^{3}}{/tex}

solution_html:
Consider a bar magnet NS of length 2l and of pole strength qm. Suppose the magnetic field is to be determined at a point P lying on the equatorial line of the magnet NS at a distance r from its centre, as shown in the figure.

Imagine a unit north-pole placed at point P. Then from Coulomb's law of magnetic forces, the force exerted by the N-pole of the magnet on unit north-pole is
FN = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{q_{m}}{x^{2}}{/tex}, along NP
Similarly, the force exerted by the S-pole of the magnet on unit north-pole is
FS = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{q_{m}}{x^{2}}{/tex}, along PS
As the magnitudes of FN and FS are equal, so their vertical components get cancelled while the horizontal components add up along PR.
Hence the magnetic field at the equatorial point P is 
Bequa = Net force on a unit N-pole placed at point P
= FN cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + FS cos {tex}\theta{/tex} 
= 2 FN cos {tex}\theta{/tex}  [{tex}\because{/tex} FN = FS]
= {tex}2 \cdot \frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{q_{m}}{x^{2}} \cdot \frac{l}{x}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} cos {tex}\theta=\frac{l}{x}{/tex}]
or Bequa = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{m}{\left(r^{2}+l^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} x = (r2 + l2)1/2]
where m = qm.2l, is the magnetic dipole moment.
Again for a short magnet, l << r, so we have
Bequa = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{m}{r^{3}}{/tex}, along PR
Clearly, the magnetic field at any equatorial point of a magnetic dipole is in the direction opposite to that of its magnetic dipole moment i.e., from N-pole to S-pole. So we can write
{tex}\vec{B}_{\text {equa }}=-\frac{\mu_{0} \vec{m}}{4 \pi r^{3}}{/tex}

question_html: Classify materials on the basis of their behaviour in a magnetic field. Under which category does iron come? How does the magnetic property of iron change with the increase of temperature?
question_text: Classify materials on the basis of their behaviour in a magnetic field. Under which category does iron come? How does the magnetic property of iron change with the increase of temperature?
explanation: Classification of magnetic materials: On the basis of their behaviour in external magnetic fields, Faraday classified the various substances into three categories: Diamagnetic substances: Diamagnetic substances are those which develop feeble magnetisation in the opposite direction of the magnetising field. Such substances are feebly repelled by magnets and tend to move from stronger to weaker parts of a magnetic field. Examples. Bismuth, copper, lead, zinc, tin, gold, silicon, nitrogen (at STP), water, sodium chloride, etc. Paramagnetic substances: Paramagnetic substances are those which develop feeble magnetisation in the direction of the magnetising field. Such substances are feebly attracted by magnets and tend to move from weaker to stronger parts of a magnetic field. Examples. Manganese, aluminium, chromium, platinum, sodium, copper chloride, oxygen (it STP), etc. Ferromagnetic substances: Ferromagnetic substances are those which develop strong magnetisation in the direction of the magnetising field. They are strongly attracted by magnets and tend to move from weaker to stronger parts of a magnetic field. Examples. Iron, cobalt, nickel, gadolinium and alloys like alnico. Iron is a ferromagnetic substance. As temperature increases, its magnetisation decreases due to the randomisation of its domains.

solution_html:
Classification of magnetic materials: On the basis of their behaviour in external magnetic fields, Faraday classified the various substances into three categories:

  1. Diamagnetic substances: Diamagnetic substances are those which develop feeble magnetisation in the opposite direction of the magnetising field. Such substances are feebly repelled by magnets and tend to move from stronger to weaker parts of a magnetic field. Examples. Bismuth, copper, lead, zinc, tin, gold, silicon, nitrogen (at STP), water, sodium chloride, etc.
  2. Paramagnetic substances: Paramagnetic substances are those which develop feeble magnetisation in the direction of the magnetising field. Such substances are feebly attracted by magnets and tend to move from weaker to stronger parts of a magnetic field. Examples. Manganese, aluminium, chromium, platinum, sodium, copper chloride, oxygen (it STP), etc.
  3. Ferromagnetic substances: Ferromagnetic substances are those which develop strong magnetisation in the direction of the magnetising field. They are strongly attracted by magnets and tend to move from weaker to stronger parts of a magnetic field. Examples. Iron, cobalt, nickel, gadolinium and alloys like alnico.

Iron is a ferromagnetic substance. As temperature increases, its magnetisation decreases due to the randomisation of its domains.

question_html: State Gauss's law of magnetism. What does it indicate?
question_text: State Gauss's law of magnetism. What does it indicate?
explanation: Gauss' theorem in magnetism states that the total magnetic flux through any closed surface is zero. Mathematically, Magnetic flux, {tex}\phi_{B}=\oint_{C} \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d S}=0{/tex} This theorem indicates that isolated magnetic poles do not exist or the magnetic poles always exist in pairs.

question_html: What are magnetic lines of force? Give their important properties.
question_text: What are magnetic lines of force? Give their important properties.
explanation: A magnetic line of force may be defined as the curve the tangent to which at any point gives the direction of the magnetic field at that point. It may also be defined as the path along which a unit north pole would tend to move if free to do so. Properties of lines of force: Magnetic lines of force are closed curves which start in air from the N-pole and end at the S-pole and then return to the N-pole through the interior of the magnet. The lines of force never cross each other. If they do so, that would mean there are two directions of the magnetic field at the point of intersection, which is impossible. They start from and end on the surface of the magnet normally. The lines of force have a tendency to contract lengthwise and expand sidewise. This explains attraction between unlike poles and repulsion between like poles. The relative closeness of the lines of force gives a measure of the strength of the magnetic field which is maximum at the poles.
